# Transcrição: 02-Sales Conversation 101

- **Fonte:** 02-Sales Conversation 101
- **Duração:** 17:12
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

## [00:00 - 03:02]

What's up guys? Alright, time to talk about my favorite topic, which is the sales process. Few quick words before we jump into this. Now number one, there's a couple of different approaches when it comes to sales. One is the relationship-based approach, which is essentially where you just start serving. You get on a call with somebody, you serve, you don't ask for a sale, you just say, hey, let's follow up, you serve again, you don't ask for a sale, and you just keep going. It's like kind of like dating, you know, when you kind of take somebody out in multiple dates. The only thing is that eventually you got to lean over and, you know, create some intimacy. So I don't want you to fall into the trap if you're going to follow the relationship-based approach of never asking for the sale because then you just become a professional volunteer. We don't want that. So if you're going to do the relationship-based approach, try it out. See what it feels like. Get some experience. But eventually say, hey, since we have a nice flow, a nice relationship here, let's actually make this official. Let's go steady. No, you're going to say something like, hey, why don't we actually make this official? And here's my program. And then you pitch the program. By then the relationship is so strong, it's kind of a no-brainer. Oh yeah, I'm getting results. Of course I love working with you. Let's do it. That's one way of doing sales. But that's not what we're going to talk about today. Today we're going to talk about what I call the leadership frame or the leadership approach to sales. This is a great approach when you have a ton of leads. In other words, you have a lot of people coming to you and saying, hey, I'm really interested to learn more about what you got. In that case, you really need to be more of a leader. And it's almost like playing the role of a doctor and just doing an intake. And we'll talk about what that looks like. Now my background is in film, which means for many years I was working with scripts and actors to dive deep and bring out authentic and powerful performances. And that is exactly what we're doing in the sales process as well. You know the difference. When you've seen a real quality actor deliver performance and it's moving emotionally, you feel moved. That's what we want to accomplish. Anytime you see an actor deliver a line and it just falls flat, it's because there's not connection to what's going on beneath the text, beneath the words. So come over here to the whiteboard. Let me show you something. What we got here is what sank the Titanic, an iceberg, right? But what makes the iceberg so powerful is the depth beneath it. If the Titanic just hit a snow cap, it would have kept going. It'd be in a museum somewhere today. However, because of the depth of what is beneath the surface, what we don't see, what we don't hear, that is what gives it its power. So think about that. The script that you're going to learn, that you're going to get is great.

## [03:02 - 06:07]

But it's only as powerful as the subtext, what's beneath that script. One other sort of frame I think is important for us to think about is the idea of the hero's journey made popular by Joseph Campbell. The idea that any sort of journey that happens emotionally, intellectually, any transformation that happens is where there is a problem and you have a hero who sets out on a journey to write that problem, to fix that problem. Now the hero has a guide in this story. You're the guide. Your prospect, your client is the hero. So your job as a guide, as a mentor, somebody who's already been on the journey yourself is to guide the hero through all the difficult challenges that they might face. Now the biggest challenge that a lot of people face is the commitment to sign up, to work with you, to pay, to actually devote the time, the energy, the effort to work towards obtaining the knowledge they experience, the results that you yourself have gotten. Your job in the sales conversation is to guide your hero through those challenges and help them to overcome it in order to become that better version of themselves. Now this is really important because again, a sales process is an emotional journey. We want to move people emotionally to take action. People buy, not logically, they buy emotionally and they back it up with logic. Of course you have some people that are a bit more logical buyers and emotional buyers, we're not going to get into that. But the idea being is that the sales process is a process. There are certain beats and each beat lines up to help somebody move into action, move into commitment and that's what we're going to go on and explore right now. So now that we understand the subtext and what goes beneath the scripts of your sales conversation, let's talk about the five stages or the five emotional beats of that conversation. The first beat, the first stage is to connect and we'll talk about what that looks like. The second is to discover. The third is to offer when you're actually offering your solution. The fourth is to coach to help somebody overcome any challenge, any objection that they might have to signing up and working with you. And lastly, the fifth is to close, close the loop, close the circle, close the deal. So let's hop on into it and get a broad stroke sense of what each stage or each phase looks like. So stage one is all about connection. The two things that are really important here are building rapport, connecting with the person

## [06:07 - 09:10]

that you're speaking with. Now there's a couple ways to do this. I like to connect with energy. So if I see somebody on a Zoom call or in person or whatever the container is and their energy is a little more contained, a little more relaxed, a little more laid back and subdued, I'm going to meet them where they're at. I'm not going to come in and start saying, hey, I'm so excited to see you and blah, blah, because they're going to get scared off. And concurrently, the same thing is true of the opposite. If somebody has a lot of energy and they're like, hey, I'm so excited to meet you, Abe, and to have this conversation, I'm not going to say, okay, great, let's talk. No, of course not. People like people who are like them. So we want to create that connection. We want to create that rapport. Jeremy is going to give you some great things to think about in connecting with your prospect. But aside from rapport, you also want to set the tone and the container for the conversation. Again, what we're talking about is leadership-based sales. You're the leader. It's your show. You're the guide. You're the mentor here. Just like a doctor is asking you questions and doing an intake, you're going to frame the conversation up the way that you want it to flow. And that could be just saying, hey, here's what's going to happen. I'm going to ask you some questions. If it feels like it's a fit, I'm going to propose a solution. All you have to say is, yes, I want to join your program or no, I don't. Either is fine as long as we can create clarity by the end of the call. Can you agree to that? Yes or no? Now, once we're finished with our connection, we're going to move on to the next part. The next part, the next phase of the conversation is your discovery phase. So in this phase, the verb that you want to use is discover. Now, here's what's really important. Again, think of the doctor. When a doctor is doing an intake, the doctor is not talking too much. A doctor is mostly asking questions. Does it hurt here? What about if I do this? How long have you had this problem? Why? A couple of things. One, you want to get a comprehensive understanding of where your prospect is at. You want to learn from them. The biggest mistake that most newbies, new people that are new to sales make is that they talk too much. And what that is is you being nervous. You're just trying to show off and say, oh, I have the best product. It's not about you. Remember, it's about your prospect. Your prospect is the hero and you're the guide. So allow them to talk. Allow them to open up to ask those questions that gets them to really inform you about what's going on and then also inform themself, not just intellectually, but emotionally. So I want you to write that down. The biggest thing, and again, Jeremy's going to give you some great questions to ask, but the biggest thing I want you to pay attention to in the discovery phase is two things. Number one, build that emotion. You might have to ask the same question multiple times because the first time you ask, hey,

## [09:10 - 12:16]

what's the problem? They might have an intellectual answer. But the more you ask it, the more layers you uncover and the deeper we go. We travel from the head to the heart, and that's what we want. The second thing that we want to do aside from building emotion is you want to create some urgency. Remember that if somebody's not in pain, then they're not going to want to buy a solution. So you want to really ask questions in such a way that helps people to build urgency. I'll give you a quick example. Once upon a time, many years ago, true story, I got bit on the leg by a bug. Now I didn't have any insurance at the time. I didn't want to go to a doctor, and it started growing. A friend of mine was like, you should do something about it. I didn't want to pay, so I didn't go anywhere. However, after about a month or so, I woke up and I couldn't move my leg. I then had to pay a lot more money for ambulance and a hospital stay and antibiotics than if I just paid for a clinical visit. In that situation, my friend should have said something like, Abe, if you don't do this, what's going to happen? You can end up in the hospital. This can happen. All of a sudden, I'm going to have that urgency, and then I'm going to want to take action. In your discovery phase, ask questions to A, build emotion, and B, create that urgency. Now, the next phase that we want to move into, once people are in a place where they're really in touch with the emotion of the challenge, and they really feel the need for an urgent solution, at that point, we're going to move into the third phase, which is the offer phase. Now, here's what I would love for you to do. I want you to have prepared some sort of 30-second or 60-second story that relates to your prospect, to what they're going through. They just bore their heart. They just told you everything of what's going on in their life, their business, their relationship, whatever the offer is that you have. You want to connect to them. You want to let them know, hey, you're not alone. I've been there. Remember, you're the guide. You're the mentor. Who was the hero? Now, you're guiding. Again, it's not about you. Don't go into a hour-long soliloquy about your life. Just spend 30 seconds saying, hey, I know exactly where you are because I was in the same spot too, but hey, I figured out a solution, and that's what I'd love to share with you. That's the first thing that we're going to do is relate. The second thing that we're going to do is ask for permission to talk about our program. Your prospect was just bearing their all, and we don't want to seamlessly or we don't want to be rude and just say, oh, I got the solution. I'm going to, no, just say, hey, is it cool with you? Is it okay if I tell you a little bit about what I think is really going to serve you? Once they say yes, and they always say yes, then you go into outlining your program and talking about the specific features and benefits of what it is that you have to offer. Again, not spending an hour pitching your program, but just a very clear, concise bullet

## [12:16 - 15:21]

points of how it is that your program specifically addresses their need. That's the second thing. First thing relate, second is outline your program. The third thing is you have to ask for the sale. You have to make an offer. Don't be indirect. Be direct and say, this is the program. This is what it costs. Would you like to do this program? Would you like to buy this? Would you like to sign up? Would you like to work together? Be very clear and very specific. The fourth stage or the fourth phase of our sales conversation is the phase where you can show up and coach. We're going to dive really deep into talking about this phase because this is often called the objection handling phase or objection handling part of the call. In other words, your prospect has a challenge. It could be belief. It could be doubt, worry, fear, time, maybe the people in their life. There could be any number of things holding people back from being an absolute yes to your offer. If they're not an absolute yes, that's fine. The point is not to shy away, but to really show up as a leader and help them. Your job is to help them shift their perspective. Again, like me with my leg, if I had shifted my perspective and realized I didn't do something about my leg, I was going to pay a lot more and lose a couple of days of my life, I'd have taken action. That's all we want to do in that fourth phase is we want to help our prospects shift their perspective from a disempowered state to an empowered state. Once you do that efficiently, once you do that in a way, in a manner that really empowers your prospect, we finally move into the fifth and the last phase, which is about closing. Closing the loop, closing the sale, closing the conversation. The first step is super simple. It's just taking payment. Believe it or not, if you've done a great discovery, if you've done a great job of presenting your offer and handling objections, taking payment is the easiest part of the entire process. You just need to have a way to do it that is easy. Make sure you handle that ahead of time. Number two is you want to talk about what are the next steps. Maybe it's scheduling the first call. Maybe it's downloading your workbook. Maybe it's having access to a course, whatever it is. You want to make it clear what the next steps are. The reason why we do this is because, again, if somebody is going to sign up and work with you, it might very well be an empowered commitment that's going to change their life. The next day, they might wake up feeling a little less empowered. You want to safeguard against somebody wanting to reverse the decision to work with you and help them to maintain that positive, empowered mindset and attitude. Having the next steps helps you to do that. The third and final thing that you want to do is what I like to call a tie down, which

## [15:21 - 17:12]

basically means that you ask your prospect, why is this the right decision for you? You tell me. You have your prospect enroll you. Sell you on why this is the best decision for them. Why? What does that do? If you tell somebody what to do, it goes in one ear and out the other. But if you ask somebody and they tell you what they want to do, what they need to do, they feel it, they embody it, it goes into their nervous system. And the next morning when somebody wakes up and says, oh my God, what did I just do? They have a way to support themselves and to say, oh no, I made a commitment to my health, to my relationship, to my business, and they're able to move forward. So in summary, the five emotional phases or beats, steps, stages of a sales conversation is number one, to connect, number two, to discover, number three, to make that offer, number four, to coach and support somebody through their challenge, through their objections, and number five is to handle the logistics of the payment, to close the deal and to close that loop so somebody can then move on with the journey that you're taking them on. Lastly, I want you to think about this. Beliefs is nothing more than a transference of belief. Your feeling of absolute certainty should overcome their doubt. Remember that. You want to be absolutely certain in your solution and what it is that you can help your clients to do. And once they feel that belief resonating, emanating from you, it is going to affect them. It's going to help them feel empowered to make that commitment to transform their life.
